school: First historically well-attested ancestor of Muhammad. He seized control of Mecca from the Khuza'a tribe and united the Quraysh clans under his leadership. He built the Dar al-Nadwa (the council house of Mecca), established the custodianship of the Ka'ba in Quraysh, and created the civic offices of Mecca that endured until the Islamic era. Ibn Ishaq calls him the first great leader of the Quraysh.

Grandfather and guardian of Prophet Muhammad. He rediscovered and redug the Zamzam well after receiving a divine dream directing him to its location. He raised Muhammad after the deaths of his father Abdullah and mother Amina. He led the Quraysh during Abraha's invasion — the Year of the Elephant (570 CE) in which the Ka'ba was miraculously protected. He died when Muhammad was approximately eight years old.
1st Rightly-Guided Caliph — Prophet's closest companion; first adult male to accept Islam; unified Arabia after the Prophet's death; known for absolute trust in God
parents: Abu Quhafa Uthman bin 'Amir / Salma Umm-ul-Khair
children: Asma' bint Abi Bakr , 'Abdullah bin Abi Bakr , 'Aisha bint Abi Bakr , 'Abdur Rahman bin Abi Bakr , Muhammad ibn Abi Bakr , Umm Khultum bint Abi Bakr
spouses: Qutaylah bint Abdul-'Uzza, Umm Ruman , Asma bint 'Umays , Habiba bint Kharijah ibn Zayd
quotes: Do not consider any act of kindness insignificant — even greeting your brother with a cheerful face.
Without knowledge, action is useless; and knowledge without action is futile.

school: 3rd Rightly-Guided Caliph — married two daughters of the Prophet; standardised the Quranic text (Uthmanic codex); expanded the Islamic empire to Afghanistan and North Africa
parents: 'Affan ibn Abi al-'As / Arwa bint Kuraiz bin Rabi'a
children: 'Abdullah bin 'Uthman , 'Abdullah bin 'Uthman(younger) , 'Aishah bint 'Uthman , Umm Aban bint 'Uthman , Umm 'Amr bint 'Uthman , 'Amr bin 'Uthman , Aban bin 'Uthman , 'Umar bin 'Uthman , Khalid bin 'Uthman , Maryam bint 'Uthman , al-Walid bin 'Uthman , Sa'id bin 'Uthman , Umm 'Uthman bint 'Uthman , Umm Khalid bint 'Uthman , Arwa bint 'Uthman , Umm Aban bint 'Uthman(younger) , Maryam bint 'Uthman (younger)
spouses: Ruqayyah bint Muhammad , Umm Kulthum bint Muhammad , Fakhitah bint Ghazwan , Ramlah bint Shayba bin Rabi'a , Umm 'Amr bint Jundub al-Dausi , Fatima bint al-Walid b. 'Abd Shams , Na'ilah bint al-Furafisah
quotes: If our hearts were truly pure, we would never have our fill of the words of God.
Do not be deceived by the good deeds of a man until you have observed what he does in moments of anger.

school: 2nd Rightly-Guided Caliph — transformed Islam into a world empire; conquered Persia, Egypt, Syria; established Islamic governance, welfare state and rule of law
parents: Khattab ibn Nufayl / Hantamah bint Hisham Makhzumi
children: ibn Umar , Hafsa bint Umar , 'Abdur Rahman bin 'Umar (Akbar) , 'Ubaidallah bin 'Umar , Zayd bin 'Umar , 'Asim bin 'Umar , 'Iyad bin 'Umar , Zaid (older) bin 'Umar , Ruqayyah bint 'Umar , Fatima bint 'Umar , 'Abdur Rahman(middle), 'Abdur Rahman(younger), Zainab bint 'Umar
spouses: Zaynab bint Maz'un , Umm Kulthum bint 'Amr (Jarwal) , Qaraybah bint Abi Umayyah al-Sugra , Jamila bint Thabit ibn Abi al-Aflah , 'Atikah bint Zayd , Umm Kulthum bint 'Ali bin Abi Talib , Umm Hakim , Umm Walad(Lihayyah), Umm Walad(Fakhihah)
quotes: Hold yourself to account before you are held to account — and weigh your deeds before they are weighed against you.
Learn Arabic, for it strengthens the mind and increases one's noble character.
The one who is most deserving of respect is the one who does not seek it.
Do not let your happiness depend on something you may lose.

school: 4th Rightly-Guided Caliph — cousin and son-in-law of the Prophet; considered the fountain of Islamic mysticism; his Nahj al-Balagha is the greatest treasury of Arabic wisdom
parents: Abu Talib ibn 'Abd al-Muttalib / Fatima bint Asad
children: Hassan ibn Ali bin Abi Talib , Hussain ibn 'Ali ibn Abi Talib , Zaynab bint 'Ali bin Abi Talib , Umm Kulthum bint 'Ali bin Abi Talib , Muhammad bin 'Ali bin al-Hanfiyyah , al-Abbas, 'Umar bin 'Ali bin Abi Talib , Ja'far, 'Uthman, Yahya (died young), Fatima bint 'Ali bin Abi Talib , others
spouses: Fatima bint Muhammad , Khawlah bint Ja'far , , Asma bint 'Umays , Umm Walad(s)
quotes: Do not be a slave to others when God has made you free.
Speak to people according to their level of understanding — do you want God and His Prophet to be denied?
The tongue is a beast — if you let it loose, it will devour you.
Two things define you: your patience when you have nothing, and your attitude when you have everything.

school: The founder and first caliph of the Umayyad dynasty, he ruled from 661 to 680 CE after a prolonged political struggle with Ali ibn Abi Talib following the assassination of Uthman. A skilled administrator and diplomat, he had previously served as governor of Syria for twenty years under the Rashidun caliphs. He transformed the caliphate into a hereditary monarchy by securing the succession of his son Yazid, a move that remained deeply controversial. His reign established Damascus as the capital and laid the administrative foundations that enabled the rapid expansion of the Umayyad state.
bio: First Umayyad Caliph (602-680), founder of the Umayyad dynasty who ruled the Islamic Empire through diplomatic skill and military campaigns. He established the administrative systems that defined early Islamic state governance.

school: Companion who served as secretary of revelation; governor of Syria for 20 years under Umar and Uthman; founded the Umayyad caliphate after the civil war; transformed the caliphate into a hereditary monarchy; opened new frontiers of Islamic expansion; his administration built the first Arab navy
parents: Abu Sufyan ibn Harb / Hind bint 'Utba b. Rabi'a b. 'Abd Shams
quotes: I do not place my sword where my tongue suffices, and I do not place my tongue where patience suffices.
I will not come between people and their tongues, so long as they do not come between us and our authority.
If there were a single hair connecting me to my people, I would not let it break.

school: The fifth Umayyad caliph ruled from 685 to 705 CE and is credited with consolidating Umayyad authority after the Second Fitna. He introduced Arabic as the official administrative language of the caliphate and reformed the coinage system, replacing Byzantine and Sassanid coins with purely Islamic currency. His reign saw the construction of the Dome of the Rock in Jerusalem, one of the earliest masterpieces of Islamic architecture.
bio: A 7th-century Umayyad caliph (685-705) who centralized Umayyad administration, standardized Islamic coinage, and patronized early Islamic ascetic movements.

school: The second Umayyad caliph ruled from 680 to 683 CE during one of the most turbulent periods in early Islamic history. His reign saw the Battle of Karbala in 680 CE, in which Husayn ibn Ali and his companions were killed, an event that became central to Shi'i identity and mourning traditions. He also faced the revolt of Abdullah ibn al-Zubayr in the Hejaz. His legacy remains deeply contested, with sharply differing assessments across Islamic sectarian traditions.
bio: Yazid I was an Umayyad Caliph whose reign is marked by conflict, particularly the Battle of Karbala and the siege of Medina. His rule is controversial in Islamic history and particularly significant in Shia Islam.

school: A powerful Umayyad governor of Iraq from 694 to 714 CE, he was instrumental in suppressing revolts and maintaining central authority during the reigns of Abd al-Malik and al-Walid I. He undertook significant administrative reforms, standardised the Arabic text of the Quran by adding diacritical marks, and founded the city of Wasit. His rule was marked by both effective governance and a reputation for harsh punishments, making him one of the most controversial figures in early Islamic history.
bio: Powerful Umayyad governor and military commander (661-714) known for his administrative reforms and brutal suppression of rebellions during the reign of Abd al-Malik. He standardized the Arabic script and was a controversial but influential figure in early Islamic history.

school: The sixth Umayyad caliph ruled from 705 to 715 CE, a period often regarded as the zenith of Umayyad power and territorial expansion. Under his reign, Muslim armies conquered the Iberian Peninsula, Sindh, and Transoxiana, bringing the caliphate to its greatest geographic extent. He commissioned the construction of the Great Mosque of Damascus and expanded the Prophet's Mosque in Medina, leaving a lasting architectural legacy.
quotes: I built the Great Mosque of Damascus to be a monument that would last until the Day of Judgment.
A caliph’s legacy is measured not by his conquests but by what he leaves for his people.

school: The eighth Umayyad caliph, he ruled from 717 to 720 CE and is often regarded as the most pious and just of the Umayyad rulers, earning the title 'the fifth Rightly Guided Caliph' among many Sunni scholars. He reversed several policies of his predecessors, abolishing the public cursing of Ali ibn Abi Talib, returning confiscated properties to the family of the Prophet, and reforming taxation to treat Muslim and non-Muslim subjects more equitably. His brief reign of less than three years was marked by austerity, administrative reform, and efforts to model governance on the example of the early caliphs.
quotes: He who knows himself to be among the worst of people cannot be proud; and he who knows his deeds may end at any moment cannot be reckless.
Do not be a helper to the oppressor — otherwise you yourself become one.
He who knows himself to be among the worst cannot be proud — and he who knows his deeds may end cannot be reckless.
The best deed is to bring joy to the heart of a believer.

school: The second Abbasid caliph ruled from 754 to 775 CE and is regarded as the true founder of the Abbasid dynasty's administrative apparatus. He established Baghdad in 762 CE as the new capital, designing it as a round city that became the intellectual and commercial centre of the Islamic world. His reign laid the institutional foundations that enabled the Abbasid golden age under his successors.
quotes: Four things sustain a kingdom: a scholar who acts on his knowledge, a judge who does not fear blame, a treasurer who is honest, and an advisor who does not flatter.
I built Baghdad in the shape of a circle so that I would be at the centre of the world.

school: Umayyad Emirate of Córdoba — introduced the Maliki school of jurisprudence to Al-Andalus; expanded Córdoba Mosque
bio: Umayyad Caliph of Córdoba (788-796), son of Abd al-Rahman II, who consolidated power in al-Andalus. His reign marked a period of relative political stability in early medieval Iberia.

school: Abbasid Caliph — ruled at the peak of the Islamic Golden Age; contemporary of Charlemagne; the protagonist of A Thousand and One Nights; patron of science, poetry and music
quotes: The caliphate is not a robe you wear — it is a burden you carry.
Knowledge is the life of the mind, and the light of every era.
I look at a cloud and say: rain wherever you wish — your harvest will come to me regardless.
The caliph who does not weep for his subjects is not worthy of them.

school: Abbasid Caliph — founded Bayt al-Hikma (House of Wisdom); commissioned mass translation of Greek science; the greatest patron of knowledge in Islamic history
quotes: Of all pleasures, the greatest is discourse with a man of learning.
The translator is the tongue of civilisation.
Knowledge is power — and I will not rest until every branch of it has been translated into Arabic.
The disagreement of scholars is a mercy — for it opens the doors of inquiry.
A ruler who does not love knowledge will be governed by those who do not deserve to govern him.

school: The tenth Abbasid caliph reigned from 847 to 861 CE and reversed the Mu'tazili theological policies of his predecessors by restoring traditionalist Sunni orthodoxy. He was a prolific builder, commissioning the Great Mosque of Samarra with its iconic spiral minaret. His assassination by his own Turkish guard in 861 CE marked the beginning of a period of military dominance over the caliphate known as the Anarchy at Samarra.
bio: Abbasid Caliph (847-861) who initiated the Mihna's final phase against Mu'tazilite theology, promoting orthodox Sunni doctrine. He was a significant but ultimately controversial figure whose reign saw both administrative competence and religious strife.

school: The founder and first caliph of the Fatimid dynasty, he claimed descent from the Prophet Muhammad through Ismail ibn Ja'far and established the Fatimid state in North Africa in 909 CE. He overthrew the Aghlabid dynasty in Ifriqiya and founded the city of Mahdia on the Tunisian coast as his capital. His reign established the Ismaili Shi'i caliphate as a major rival to the Abbasid caliphate in Baghdad.
bio: Ubayd Allah al-Mahdi was the founder of the Fatimid Caliphate in North Africa who claimed descent from the Prophet Muhammad through his daughter Fatima. He established the Fatimid dynasty as a major Islamic power in the Mediterranean.

school: A military commander of Sicilian origin who served the Fatimid dynasty, he led the conquest of Egypt in 969 CE and founded the city of Cairo as the new Fatimid capital. He established Al-Azhar Mosque, which later became one of the most prestigious centres of Islamic learning in the world. His administrative and military achievements were foundational to Fatimid power in North Africa and the eastern Mediterranean.
bio: Fatimid general and administrator (911-992), a Sicilian slave who rose to prominence under the Fatimid caliphs and served as chief military commander. He was instrumental in Fatimid military campaigns and governance in North Africa.

school: The Hamdanid emir of Aleppo from 944 to 967 CE, he was renowned both as a warrior against the Byzantine Empire and as a lavish patron of Arabic literature and scholarship. His court attracted some of the greatest figures of the Islamic golden age, including the poet al-Mutanabbi and the philosopher al-Farabi. Despite frequent military setbacks against the Byzantines, his cultural patronage left a lasting legacy on Arabic letters.
bio: Sayf al-Dawla was a 10th-century Arab ruler who founded the Hamdanid dynasty and is famous for his military campaigns against Byzantine forces in northern Syria. He is celebrated in Arabic literature and poetry as a champion of Islam against Byzantine expansion.

school: Ghaznavid Sultan — first to use the title Sultan; invaded India 17 times; patron of Al-Biruni and Ferdowsi; spread Islam deep into South Asia
quotes: The sword of my justice is sharper than the sword of my army.
A king who does not love scholars does not love his own kingdom.
The sword of my justice is sharper than the sword of my army.
A king who does not love scholars does not love his own kingdom.

school: Almoravid dynasty founder — united Morocco and al-Andalus; defeated Alfonso VI at Battle of Sagrajas; patron of Islamic scholarship
bio: Yusuf ibn Tashfin was an Almoravid leader who founded the city of Marrakech and led the Almoravid dynasty in North Africa and Spain. He is remembered for his military campaigns against Christian kingdoms and his role in establishing Almoravid dominance.

school: Seljuk Vizier — the greatest medieval Islamic administrator; founded the Nizamiyya universities (where Al-Ghazali taught); author of the Siyasatnama
quotes: A kingdom can endure with unbelief, but it cannot endure with injustice.
The stability of the state rests on the quality of its servants — choose them for merit, not for favour.

school: The second sultan of the Great Seljuk Empire ruled from 1063 to 1072 CE and dramatically altered the geopolitical landscape of the medieval Middle East. His decisive victory over the Byzantine Emperor Romanos IV at the Battle of Manzikert in 1071 opened Anatolia to Turkic settlement and ultimately led to the foundation of the Sultanate of Rum. He was known for his military prowess and his reliance on the brilliant vizier Nizam al-Mulk.
quotes: If I am victorious, it is by the will of God — if I fall, let my son be your sultan.
I fought at Manzikert with my shroud tied to my horse — for I was prepared to meet God.

school: An eleventh-century Ismaili da'i (missionary) of Persian origin, he founded the Nizari Ismaili state and established its headquarters at the mountain fortress of Alamut in northern Iran in 1090 CE. He developed a network of remote fortresses across Persia and Syria and employed a strategy of targeted assassination against political and military opponents, giving rise to the legendary reputation of the Assassins in both Islamic and European sources. His movement survived for over 150 years until the Mongol destruction of Alamut in 1256 CE.
bio: Nizari Ismaili missionary and founder of the Alamut fortress state (1050-1124), establishing an influential esoteric Islamic community in Persia. He is often associated with the early Assassins, though this characterization is debated by scholars.

school: Zengid ruler — the predecessor and patron of Salahuddin; united Syria and prepared the Islamic world for the Crusade counter-offensive; known for extraordinary personal piety
quotes: I am ashamed to ask God for victory when my private life is not fit to be seen.
What use is the conquest of Jerusalem if I have not first conquered my own desires?

school: Mali Imperial Court — defeated Sosso king Sumanguru Kante at Kirina 1235; founded largest West African empire
bio: Sunjata Keïta was a legendary West African ruler credited with founding the Mali Empire in the 13th century. He is celebrated in oral tradition and historical accounts as a military commander who unified Mandinka peoples and established Mali as a powerful empire.

school: Nasrid Dynasty — founder; began construction of the Alhambra; adopted the motto 'Wa la ghalib illa Allah' (No victor but God)
bio: Founder of the Nasrid dynasty in Granada (1194-1273), establishing an Islamic kingdom in al-Andalus that would survive until 1492. He was instrumental in preserving Islamic civilization in medieval Iberia during Christian reconquest.

school: A Mamluk sultan of Egypt and Syria who ruled from 1260 to 1277 CE, he rose from enslaved Kipchak origins to become one of the most formidable military leaders of the medieval Islamic world. He played a crucial role in the Mamluk victory over the Mongols at the Battle of Ain Jalut in 1260, halting their westward advance. His campaigns systematically dismantled the remaining Crusader states along the Levantine coast and fortified the Mamluk state against external threats.
quotes: A ruler who does not know his enemy has already lost.
Speed is half of victory — the enemy who does not see you coming is already defeated.

school: Ottoman Empire founder — established the Ottoman state in 1299; his dream of a spreading tree is the founding myth of the Ottomans
bio: Osman I was the founder of the Ottoman Empire who established an independent principality in Anatolia in the late 13th century. He is the eponymous founder from whom the Ottoman dynasty takes its name.

school: A Turco-Mongol conqueror who founded the Timurid Empire in the late fourteenth century, he launched devastating military campaigns from Anatolia to India, conquering vast territories. His campaigns were marked by both extraordinary military brilliance and extreme destruction, including the sacking of Delhi, Damascus, and Baghdad. Despite the devastation wrought by his conquests, the Timurid dynasty he established became a major patron of art, architecture, and scholarship, particularly under his successors in Samarkand and Herat.
quotes: It is better to be on the move than to stand still — for the standing water becomes stagnant.
Whoever has no compassion for the living deserves no mercy from history.

school: A Timurid queen and patron of the arts, she was the wife of Shah Rukh and one of the most influential women in Islamic history. She commissioned some of the finest architectural works of the Timurid period, including the Musalla complex in Herat and the Goharshad Mosque in Mashhad. Her patronage helped establish Herat as a major centre of Persian culture, art, and scholarship during the fifteenth century.
bio: Timurid queen and patron of arts (1378-1457), wife of Shahrokh and mother of Baysunghur, renowned for her architectural commissions and support of cultural development in Herat. She was one of the most influential women in the Timurid dynasty.

school: Timurid sultan and astronomer-king (1394–1449) who built the great observatory at Samarkand, producing the most accurate star catalogue of the pre-telescopic age. His Zij-i Sultani mapped over 1,000 stars with unprecedented precision. Murdered by his own son, his legacy endures in the astronomical tables that European scientists relied on for two centuries.
quotes: Religions disperse like mist — kingdoms perish — but the works of the scholar remain for all time.
The astronomer who does not observe the sky with his own eyes trusts in shadows.

school: Ottoman Dynasty — sixth sultan; twice abdicated; defeated last major Crusade at Varna 1444; father of Mehmed II
bio: Murad II was an Ottoman Sultan who strengthened the Ottoman Empire and fought against Hungarian and Venetian forces to expand Ottoman territory. He is notable for his administrative reforms and military campaigns in the Balkans.

school: Mamluk Sultanate — greatest builder among Mamluk sultans; his citadel in Alexandria was built on the site of the ancient Pharos lighthouse
bio: Qaitbay was a Mamluk Sultan of Egypt who strengthened the Mamluk state militarily and culturally during his reign. He is remembered for his extensive building projects, including the Citadel of Qaitbay in Alexandria.

school: Ottoman Sultan — conquered Constantinople in 1453 ending the Byzantine Empire; spoke 6 languages; patron of scholars from across the world; quoted the hadith 'What a fine commander will conquer Constantinople'
quotes: A ruler without learned men is like a man without a soul.
I have now decided to transfer the Roman Empire from Constantinople to Istanbul.

school: Timurid School — his court in Herat was the cultural capital of the Islamic world; patron of Jami, Nava'i, and Bihzad
bio: Timurid sultan of Herat (1438-1506) known as a patron of arts, literature, and science, supporting scholars like Jami and Mir Ali Shir Nava'i. His court became a major cultural center of the Renaissance in Central Asia.

school: Ottoman Dynasty — eighth sultan; accepted 100,000+ Jews expelled from Spain; known for Sufi piety; abdicated to Selim I
quotes: The scholar is more precious to the state than the soldier — the soldier protects the body of the empire, the scholar protects its soul.
Open your ports — for the one who flees persecution carries knowledge with him.
Justice delayed is the seed of rebellion.

school: The ninth Ottoman sultan, he reigned from 1512 to 1520 and dramatically expanded the empire's eastern and southern frontiers. His decisive victory over the Safavids at the Battle of Chaldiran in 1514 secured eastern Anatolia, and his subsequent conquest of the Mamluk Sultanate in 1516-1517 brought Egypt, Syria, and the Hejaz under Ottoman control. With the acquisition of the holy cities of Mecca and Medina, the Ottoman sultans assumed the role of protectors of the Two Holy Mosques and claimed leadership of the broader Sunni Muslim world.
quotes: I prefer the company of scholars to the company of kings.
The greatest victory is the one that requires no sword.
Whoever serves knowledge serves the eternal — whoever serves only power serves dust.

school: An Ottoman admiral and corsair of the sixteenth century, he established Ottoman naval supremacy in the Mediterranean and served as the Kapudan Pasha (Grand Admiral) of the Ottoman fleet. He captured Algiers and placed it under Ottoman suzerainty, becoming its governor and a key figure in the expansion of Ottoman influence in North Africa. His victory over the Holy League at the Battle of Preveza in 1538 secured Ottoman dominance of the Mediterranean for over three decades.
bio: Ottoman naval commander and corsair (1478-1546) who dominated the Mediterranean and served as Kapudan Pasha (Ottoman High Admiral). He is celebrated in Ottoman history as a legendary naval warrior and defender against European powers.

school: Mughal founder — descended from Timur and Genghis Khan; founded the Mughal Empire in 1526; wrote the Baburnama, the first autobiography in Islamic literature
quotes: A warrior who is also a poet has two weapons — the sword for the body and the word for the soul.
In the hour of defeat, the noble man plans his return; the coward makes excuses.
The garden is the proof that beauty and order are not opposites.

school: Sur Empire — Afghan ruler who defeated Humayun and ruled North India; Grand Trunk Road builder; his admin system adopted by Akbar
bio: Sher Shah Suri was an Afghan ruler who briefly displaced the Mughal Empire and implemented significant administrative and military reforms. He is noted for his organized revenue system and infrastructure development, including the Grand Trunk Road.

school: Shah Ismail I (1487–1524) was the founder of the Safavid dynasty and the ruler who united Iran under Twelver Shia Islam, reshaping the religious and political landscape of the Islamic world. Born in Ardabil to a family of Sufi shaykhs who led the militant Safaviyya order, he began his military campaign at age fourteen and conquered Tabriz by sixteen, declaring himself shah. He proclaimed Twelver Shiism the state religion — a revolutionary act that transformed Iran's identity and set it apart from the Sunni Ottoman and Mughal empires. He was also a poet of distinction, writing mystical verse in Azerbaijani Turkish under the pen name Khatai. His defeat by the Ottomans at the Battle of Chaldiran in 1514 ended his territorial expansion but not his dynasty, which ruled Iran for over two centuries.
bio: Shah Ismail I was the founder of the Safavid Empire in Persia who established Twelver Shia Islam as the state religion. He is remembered for his military conquests and the establishment of a distinct Persian-Islamic state.

school: Ottoman Sultan — longest-reigning Ottoman sultan (46 years); expanded the empire to its greatest extent; commissioned Sinan to build Suleymaniye Mosque; patron of law, art and poetry
quotes: The people think of wealth and power as the greatest fate — but in this world a spell of health is the best state.
My horse is my witness: I have ruled an empire, and yet I have not found a single day of true contentment.

school: Mughal Empire — son of Babur; lost and regained his empire; his mausoleum is the prototype for the Taj Mahal
quotes: The man who falls and rises is greater than the man who never falls.
Learning is the only kingdom that cannot be taken by force.
My library is my most faithful companion — it has never abandoned me.

school: Jalal al-Din Muhammad Akbar (1542–1605), the greatest Mughal emperor, who expanded the empire across most of the Indian subcontinent. His policy of Sulh-i Kul (universal peace) promoted tolerance among Hindus, Muslims, Jains, and Christians.
quotes: A monarch should be ever intent on conquest — otherwise his neighbours rise in arms against him.
The wise ruler takes counsel with the learned in every matter before he acts.

school: Mughal Empire — fourth emperor; renowned for justice; pioneered Mughal miniature painting; Chain of Justice
bio: Mughal Emperor of India (1569-1627) known for his patronage of arts, architecture, and gardens, continuing the cultural flourishing of the Mughal Empire. His reign saw significant development of Mughal miniature painting and the expansion of imperial authority.
quotes: In the year of my life, I became disgusted with the world and everything in it

school: The fifth Safavid shah ruled Iran from 1588 to 1629 and transformed it into one of the most powerful empires of the early modern world. He reformed the military by creating a standing army equipped with gunpowder weapons, recaptured lost territories from the Ottomans and Uzbeks, and moved the capital to Isfahan, transforming it into a city of legendary beauty. His reign is considered the apex of the Safavid dynasty and a high point of Persian art, architecture, and trade.
quotes: The king who does not know his people does not know his kingdom.
I built Isfahan not for myself but for the world — let every visitor know what Persia is.
Justice is the pillar of the state — remove it and the roof falls on everyone.

school: A seventeenth-century Mughal empress, she was the wife of Emperor Jahangir and one of the most powerful women in Mughal history. Born Mehr-un-Nissa to a Persian immigrant family, she effectively co-ruled the empire during her husband's later years, issuing imperial orders, minting coins in her own name, and commanding influence over court appointments. She was also a patron of architecture, gardens, and trade, and her political acumen shaped Mughal court politics for over a decade.
bio: Nur Jahan was a Mughal empress and wife of Emperor Jahangir who wielded significant political and cultural influence in the Mughal court. She is noted for her patronage of arts, architecture, and gardens, and her role in state affairs.

school: Ottoman Dynasty — fourteenth sultan; built Sultan Ahmed Mosque (Blue Mosque); signed Treaty of Zsitvatorok
bio: Ottoman Sultan (1603-1617) who commissioned the construction of the Blue Mosque in Istanbul, one of the masterpieces of Ottoman architecture. His reign marked a period of relative stability during the early 17th century Ottoman Empire.

school: The fifth Mughal emperor ruled from 1628 to 1658 and presided over what is often considered the golden age of Mughal architecture. He commissioned the Taj Mahal in memory of his wife Mumtaz Mahal, along with the Red Fort and Jama Masjid in Delhi. His later years were overshadowed by a war of succession among his sons, and he was deposed and imprisoned by his son Aurangzeb until his death in 1666.
bio: Shah Jahan was a Mughal Emperor renowned for commissioning the Taj Mahal and other magnificent architectural projects that represent the height of Mughal art and culture. He expanded Mughal territory and consolidated Mughal power in India.

school: The sixth Mughal emperor ruled from 1658 to 1707 CE, presiding over the empire at its greatest territorial extent while also sowing the seeds of its eventual decline. A devout Muslim who compiled the Fatawa-e-Alamgiri, a comprehensive compendium of Hanafi jurisprudence, he expanded the empire deep into the Deccan through decades of costly military campaigns. His reign remains deeply contested: praised by some for his personal piety and legal scholarship, and criticised by others for policies that alienated Hindu subjects and strained the empire's administrative cohesion.
quotes: I came alone into this world and I leave alone — yet I did not realise until now how alone I truly am.
I have sinned terribly — I do not know what punishment awaits me.

school: An Iranian ruler who founded the Afsharid dynasty and reigned from 1736 to 1747, he is often called the last great Asian conqueror. Rising from humble Turkmen origins, he expelled the Afghan Hotaki dynasty, defeated the Ottomans, and invaded Mughal India, sacking Delhi in 1739. His military genius was matched by a turbulent later reign marked by increasing paranoia and cruelty, ending with his assassination by his own officers.
bio: Nadir Shah was a Persian military commander and ruler who founded the Afsharid dynasty and is remembered for his conquests extending from India to the Ottoman Empire. He is celebrated for briefly restoring Persian imperial power in the early 18th century.

school: The 34th Ottoman sultan reigned from 1876 to 1909, presiding over a period of rapid modernisation alongside growing nationalist pressures. He oversaw the construction of the Hejaz Railway connecting Damascus to Medina and expanded the telegraph network across the empire. His centralised autocratic rule drew opposition from the Young Turks, who eventually deposed him in 1909. He remains a polarising figure, admired by some for his pan-Islamic diplomacy and criticised by others for his authoritarian methods.
bio: The 34th Ottoman Sultan (1876-1909) whose reign encompassed constitutional reforms, modernization efforts, and the decline of Ottoman power amid nationalist movements and imperial wars.

school: Senussi Order — leader of the Senussi Sufi order; first and only King of Libya 1951–1969; deposed by Gaddafi while abroad
bio: First king of independent Libya (1889-1983), reigning from 1951 to 1969 before being deposed in a military coup. He was a prominent Islamic and nationalist leader who guided Libya through its transition to independence.

school: Founder of the Islamic Republic of Iran (1902–1989) and leader of the 1979 revolution that overthrew the Shah. A senior Twelver Shia cleric and marja, he developed the theory of Wilayat al-Faqih (Guardianship of the Jurist), making the supreme religious scholar the head of state. His revolution reshaped Middle Eastern politics and Shia political thought worldwide.
bio: Iranian Islamic revolutionary and Shia cleric (1902-1989) who led the Iranian Revolution of 1979 and established the Islamic Republic of Iran. He developed the concept of Wilayat al-Faqih (guardianship of the jurist) and became the Supreme Leader of Iran.
quotes: Do not wait for orders from above. Do your duty and be responsible
A revolution is not made; it is
The Quran should be recited so that you weep, and if you do not weep then recite until you do weep

school: First democratically elected president of Indonesia (1999–2001) and longtime chairman of Nahdlatul Ulama, the world’s largest Islamic organization with over 40 million members. Known as ‘Gus Dur,’ he championed religious pluralism, interfaith dialogue, and democracy within an Islamic framework. A scholar of both classical Arabic texts and Western philosophy.
bio: Indonesian Islamic scholar and the fourth president of Indonesia (1999-2001), known for his moderate interpretations of Islam and advocacy for pluralism and democracy. He was a prominent leader of Nahdlatul Ulama, one of the world's largest Islamic organizations.

school: President of Iran (1997–2005) who championed the ‘Dialogue Among Civilizations’ at the United Nations. A Shia cleric trained in philosophy, he pushed for press freedom, civil society, and rule of law within the Islamic Republic. His reformist agenda inspired a generation of Iranian intellectuals, though it was ultimately constrained by conservative opposition.
bio: Iranian philosopher and politician who served as President of Iran (1997-2005), known for his advocacy of dialogue among civilizations and reform-oriented policies. He was influential in promoting a more moderate approach to governance and international relations.

school: Arwa al-Sulayhi was the queen of Yemen who ruled independently for over fifty years, making her one of the longest-reigning female sovereigns in Islamic history. She was a patron of learning and architecture, moved the capital to Jibla, and maintained the Fatimid Ismaili da'wa in Yemen. She is one of only two women in Islamic history whose name was mentioned in the Friday khutba.
bio: Yemeni Tayyibi Ismaili queen (1048-1138) who ruled as regent and sovereign, known for her architectural patronage and political acumen. She was one of the most powerful women rulers in medieval Islamic history.

school: Abdulaziz ibn Abdul Rahman Al Saud (Ibn Saud) was the founder and first king of the modern Kingdom of Saudi Arabia. Through a series of military campaigns beginning with the recapture of Riyadh in 1902, he unified most of the Arabian Peninsula under his rule by 1932. His alliance with the Wahhabi religious establishment shaped the kingdom's religious identity.
bio: Founder of the modern Kingdom of Saudi Arabia (1876-1953), unifying the Arabian Peninsula under Wahhabi Islam through military campaigns and strategic alliances. He established the Saud dynasty that continues to rule Saudi Arabia.

school: Idris ibn Abdallah was a descendant of Ali ibn Abi Talib who fled the Abbasid persecution and founded the Idrisid dynasty in Morocco, the first Islamic state in the Maghreb independent of the Abbasids. His arrival in 788 established a lasting connection between the sharifi lineage and Moroccan political culture. His son Idris II founded the city of Fez.
bio: Founder of the Idrisid dynasty in Morocco (745-791), establishing the first independent Islamic state in the Maghreb and founding the city of Fez. He was an Alid and a significant early figure in the Moroccan Islamic tradition.

school: Muhammad of Ghor (Mu'izz al-Din Muhammad) was the Ghurid sultan whose conquests established lasting Muslim political power in northern India. His victory at the Second Battle of Tarain in 1192 led to the founding of the Delhi Sultanate by his general Qutb al-Din Aybak. His campaigns reshaped the political and religious landscape of the Indian subcontinent.
bio: Muhammad of Ghor was a 12th-13th century ruler who founded the Ghurid Empire and played a crucial role in the Islamic conquest and establishment of Muslim rule in northern India. He is known for his military campaigns and the expansion of Islamic sovereignty in the Indian subcontinent.

school: Murad I was the third Ottoman sultan who transformed a small Anatolian principality into a Balkan empire. He conquered Thrace, Macedonia, and Serbia, established the devshirme system, and created the Janissary corps. His death at the Battle of Kosovo in 1389, where he defeated a coalition of Balkan states, became a legendary moment in both Ottoman and Serbian history.
bio: Murad I was the third Ottoman Sultan who expanded Ottoman territory significantly in the Balkans and consolidated Ottoman power in Anatolia and Europe. He is remembered for his military reforms and the expansion of Ottoman dominance in southeastern Europe.

school: Al-Mansur Qalawun was a Mamluk sultan of Egypt whose reign marked the consolidation of Mamluk power. He defeated the Mongols, expelled the last Crusader outposts, and founded a dynasty that ruled for over a century. His great hospital-madrasa complex on Cairo's Bayn al-Qasrayn remains one of the finest examples of Mamluk architecture.
bio: Qalawun was a Mamluk Sultan of Egypt who significantly expanded Mamluk territorial control and power in the eastern Mediterranean. He is notable for his military victories against the Crusaders and his architectural patronage.

school: Abu al-Abbas al-Saffah (722–754) was the first Abbasid caliph, whose revolution in 750 CE overthrew the Umayyad dynasty and inaugurated the Abbasid era that would last five centuries. A descendant of the Prophet's uncle al-Abbas ibn Abd al-Muttalib, he rallied support from disaffected groups in Khorasan under the black banners of the Abbasid movement. His brief four-year reign was marked by the consolidation of power and the systematic elimination of Umayyad rivals. He established his capital at Kufa before his early death, after which his brother al-Mansur built Baghdad and secured the dynasty's permanence.
bio: First Abbasid Caliph who ruled from 750-754 CE, establishing the Abbasid Caliphate after overthrowing the Umayyad dynasty. Known for consolidating Abbasid power and implementing administrative reforms.

school: Al-Mustansir II (d. 1261) was the first Abbasid caliph of Cairo, installed by the Mamluk sultan Baybars in 1261 to restore a symbolic caliphate after the Mongol destruction of Baghdad in 1258. A member of the Abbasid family who escaped the fall of Baghdad, he was recognised as Commander of the Faithful and legitimised Baybars' rule in return. He died during an ill-fated attempt to recapture Baghdad from the Mongols. The Cairo Abbasid caliphate he inaugurated continued as a ceremonial institution under Mamluk protection until the Ottoman conquest of Egypt in 1517.
bio: Abbasid Caliph of Cairo who ruled in the 13th century during the Mamluk period. He served as a figurehead caliph with limited temporal authority.

school: Mansa Musa I (c. 1280–c. 1337) was the emperor of the Mali Empire and is widely regarded as one of the wealthiest individuals in history. His legendary 1324 pilgrimage to Mecca, accompanied by a caravan of tens of thousands bearing vast quantities of gold, caused inflation in Cairo's gold markets and put the Mali Empire on European maps — notably the 1375 Catalan Atlas, which depicts him holding a gold nugget. Under his rule, Timbuktu became a major centre of Islamic learning, and the Djinguereber Mosque was built with the architect al-Sahili whom Musa brought back from his hajj. His reign marks the zenith of West African Islamic civilisation.
bio: Mali Empire ruler (1280-1337) whose pilgrimage to Mecca in 1324 became legendary for its wealth and splendor, establishing Mali as a major Islamic power in West Africa. He was a significant patron of Islamic learning and architecture in Timbuktu.
